THE JAPANESE SITUATION".
Owing to our intiinaU' illations
with Japan and the fact that our
prosperity is now dependant on
Japanese subjects, and another
fact, that we are calling fu more
of them every day, it is supremely
important that we should under
stand, if it is possible to do so, the
general course of political
thought -and action in that coun
try. We are now involved in one
rather serious dispute with the
Japanese and it is evident enough
that there will be a good deal
more friction in the future, with
or without annexation.
The resignation of Count
Okuma from the otlice of Foreign
Affairs in Japan was expected. It
appears to be due to the action of
the Progressionist's party of
which Count Okuma is the leader.
That party demanded at tirst a
great increase in the military and
naval strength of Japan, and its
pronounced foreign policy was
the closing out ot ine territorial
jurisdiction of foreign powers in
the Empire. At no time was the
acquisition of territory beyond
the sea advocated by that party
III lilt? I-Jlv. L Ul UUl Ul 11. XMK, l"
yearly maintenance of these great
armaments has already caused a
delict of over $20,000,000 and the
Progressionists, afraid of heir
own policy, now demand great re
forms and economies in the ad
ministration by the Cabinet and
. it declares that its promises have
not been kept. Count Okuma, as
the leader of that party, had been
taken into the Cabinet in order to
keep the party quiet. He is not a
Satsuma Inan, and as the Oo
ernment is in the hands of the
Satsuma clan, he was not in much
1. -w-w J 11 "I
favor. with it. lie sougnt tne aiu
of Count Ito who is in sympathy
with the Liberals, but he got no
aid. He was then forced to aban
don his own party or resign. He
resigned. The jingoes here have
it in their heads that the Hawai
ian matter had something to do
with it. It had about as much to
do with it as it had to do with the
late Presidential contest between
Dry an and McKinley.
In neither the vernacular or the
foreign Press of Japan is the Ha
waiian matter mentioned. The
Japanese Mail gives eight
columns to the discussion of the
situation, and the Kobe Chroni
cle several colums. Xo allusion
is made to the Hawaiian affair.
The issue is stated by them to be
the alarming financial condition
of the Government, and of course,
with that is the struggle for
power between the clans.
In order to meet the alarming
deficit, there must be increased
taxation. The leaders of the
many factions are afraid to meet
that issue, because any party that
advocates it may be defeated in
the coming election. The Japan
ese peojde have had their military
and naval hula, and now don't
wish to pay for the music. This con-
Ull lull I llllio -W-l-VV H'tlf, -
fore the expulsion of immigrants
from Hawaii. When that expul
sion took place, the people acted
precisely as the " Americans act
ed when the British demanded
Mason and Slidell in 18(52.
Though thev did not have war
ships enough to blockade the
Southern ports, and General Lee
held their armies in check within
sight of the Capitol, they wanted
to fight the British at once. There
was a frenzy for fighting. But
the Government said "no"
and gave up the captured diplo
mats. So the Japanese people, in
n frenzv about what thev believed
was an insult given to the nation
by Hawaii, demanded extreme
measures. But the Government,
"conservatism itself, compared
vwithlthe people" as Mr. Mclvor
stated during his visit here, said
"no" and kept the jingoes well in
hand. j
We believe in the domination
of American civilization here.
We know also that American
prosperity here, is now, unfor
tunately, dependent upon the
Japanese, and we are quite stupid
enough to think that it is just as
well not to irritate a class of men.
on whom we are dependent, who
utnumber the Anglo-Saxons
live to one. and whose children
torn on these Islands already out
nitnber the children of Ameri
cans, h,nglisii and dermans.
However, we may not understand
the lirettv traine of iriuvhi" with
ire. American warships may
protect this city, but they cannot
protect the cam1 fields.
voyage on the cruiser Eonnie Dundee,
notices the complete obliteration of
any . distinction between line
and staff. Indeed, such .
the perfect and concentrated organi
zation of the whole service on that
vessel, we are justified in saying that
resident Dole has finally convinced
he American naval authorities that
the distinction between line and staff
should be abolished, and therefore it
will be.
:S TDD3
(D
LINE AND STAFF.
The Navy Department in Washing
ton it is said, has finally arranged the
ong standing dispute between the
line and the staff, and will recommend
the enactment of a law adjusting pro
motion and rank. The administration
of the service has been to some ex
tent, carried on under the theory of
caste in India, where one man would
bring a dish from the kitchen to the
dining room door, but could not pass
it. Then another coolie took the dish
to the table, but could not place it be
fore a guest. Another coolie took it
and put it in. its proper place. If there
were twenty servants in attendance,
only the one with the right caste could
open the wine. If the coolie who plac
ed the dishes before the guests had a
fit, the guests had to wait until an
other person of the right caste might
be found in the vicinity, before they
were served. It is claimed that this
caste theory prevailed in the Navy, be
tween the line and staff.
As this important reformation has
taken place, only since the present
squadron under Admiral Miller arriv
ed here, it would be only justice to say
that the admirable management of our
own navy or "cruisers" has not escap
ed Admiral Miller's attention, and his
report on the subject has stimulated
the Navy Department in Washington
to recommend the Hawaiian , system
Anyone who is betrayed into taking a
People who pay for seats at the
theatre have the right to be indignant
when women wear high hats and ob
struct their view. A cynical theatre
goer in one of the large cities said:
'The woman with the high hat is in
a bad way. She knows that she ob
structs the view of those behind her.
She knows that the woman in front ot
her obstructs her view with a high
hat. Wrhat should she do? She cams
to see the. play and also to be seen.
She may be seen, but she cannot see.
and is like the donkey between two
bundles of hay." A Western manager-
tried to cure the evil by hanging up a
placard near the door: "All ladies who
are not bald headed will please remove
hats." He became a martyr to the
cause. io doubt it is inconvenient to
remove hats, but that is no excuse for
selfishness. In places, where the thea
tre or the opera is a part of the daily
ife of the people, the wearing of hats
regulates itself, and women would as
quickly raise umbrellas to obscure the
view of those seated behind then, as
they would to wear high hats.

The contract arranged between the
Canadian and Australian governments
and the Union Steamship Company of
New Zealand for a monthly mail

carried on. has been signed by the
Postmaster-General of Australia, ac
cording to advices just received from

government will pay 26,626 yearly for
the service. Theconditions of the new
contract are identical with those which
expired on August 1 last.

Hilonian's schedule, which is arranged
on a 2f-day basis. As the Wilhelmina
is a big ship, w'.th extra large cargo
epace, she will require all the time pos
sible to empty and load her holds. Im
mediately upon the arrival of the wil
helmina at San Francisco, the Hilonian
will be put on the Seattle-Taeoma-Ho-nolulu
route. She will leave Seattle
for Honolulu every twenty days, al
ternating with the steamer Enterprise.
Their route will be from Seattle and
Tacoma to Honolulu, Kahului, Port Al
len, Honolulu, and thence direct to San
Francisco. '
The Lurline will at that .time be put
on the run from San Francisco to Ho
nolulu and Hilo. returning from, the
latter port to Honolulu and sailing
hence for San Francisco, alternating in
a direct service between San Francisco
and Honolulu with the Wilhelmina.
It is expected that a number of Cal
ifornia Shriners will corne to Honolulu
on the first trip of the Wilhelmina.

Harriman Ships and Chinese.
NEW YORK, November 29. Coolie
erews on Paeific Mail steamships con
trolled by the Harriman systems and
on steamships running from Puget
Sound to China, controlled by James
J. Hill, will be denounced at the fourth
annual convention of the International
Seamen's Union of America, which
opened in New York today.
Andrew Furuseth of San Francisco,
the president, said, the union was mak
ing a great effort to foster legislation
that would prevent the hiring of cheap
and inexperienced labor, particularly
coolie labor, in the American merchant
marine.
"Under existing conditions," he
said, "American sailors on the Pacific
barkentine Marv Winkelman, which ran
aground after missing her stays en
route to sea under sail, was floated off
and towed to an anchorage in the
upper bay. Investigation on the part
of Captain Generaux, representing the
board of marine underwriters, proved
the vessel received no damage from
her imprisonment in the mud bank, and
she was permitted to proceed. The
Winkelman carries a cargo of lumber
laden at Mukilteo and destined for de
livery in the Fiji Islands.
Old Whaler Pan.
NEW BEDFORD, Massachusetts, No
vember 29. After a career of seventy
four years on the high seas, first as a
Baltimore packet, then as the first
whaler sailing from this port, fliebark
Canton has sailed her final voyage. A
cablegram received here todav by her
owners stated that the vessel had been
wrecked on the island of Maio, one of
the Cape Verde group. The crew was
saved.

Opposed to those " who believe or
hope that Spain will emerge from her.
troubles .with a more stable govern
ment a writer iu the Fortnightly, who
signs himself "A Spaniard,** and who
evidently knows his country well,
draws a gloomy picture of what will
happen ufter peace. The burden of .
taxation will be frightful. The public
debt, swollen by the expenses of war,
will foot up to more than $5M:<;().t:on.tM:o,
the annual interest charge on which
will amount to $14(1 (iUt).(hiO. Yet the
citire national revenue in prosperous
?years has not bebn more than $150,000.
01)0. Repudiation or bankruptcj- lies
clearly in these figures. Then there
will follow the destruction of those
industries which the colonies have
been made to support, turning thou
sands of operatives out of employ ment.
More than 100.000 soldiers will be like
wise set adrift, and 30.000 oflicers put
on the retired list, where the pay of a
captain (if he is lucky enough to get
it ut all) is about GO cents n day. When
all these helpless multitudes begin to
teel the pinch of hunger, then, says this
Spanish writer the "tocsin of resolu
tion" will sound and it will depend on
the army whether Spain shall be C'arl
ist or republican. In any case, he
thinks it out of the power of any party
"to extricate mv unfortunate, much
beloved firnin from the abyss on the
edge of which rhe is now tottering."
"Spain makes tier melancholy bow to
tlic vast continent which her enter
prise pnee opened up to the world and
exclaims:: 'Moritiira te saluto.'"

j

A year or two ngo an artist from San
Francisco who wore a glass eye came
to Yokohama and established himself
in a little bungalow on the outskirts
of the ci:,;. > ays tlie Forkvilie Yicnai.
The weather was extremely v.arn.
and before the stranger had brcrm;

looked over the applicants, and finally
selected un old man who brought ex
cellent recommendations from his last
employer, v
When It was time to retire the artist
took out' his glass eye. laid'it on *
stand at his bedside and went to bed.
The old man picked up bis fan and the
San Francisco man was soon asleep.
Ile.slept peacefully for an hour or two,
when he was awakened by a chorus of
buzzing insects about his head. He
looked about him. and found that the
man whom he had hired to fan him was
gone.
The next morning when he went in
search of another coolie he was
amazed to discover that no orte would
work for him. lie was looked upon as
a wizard and worker of miracles, with
whom it was unsafe to be alone. The
old man hud gone among his filends
and told how the Californiar. had
taken out his eye at night and laid
it ca a stand in order that he might
watch hi? servant at night add set thfcl
he kept his fan In motion. The old
coolie's story created such excitement
that the flan Francisco man was never
| able to get another Japanese to fan
, him after that.

Farmers Union Adopts P
Hears World-Famous Speakers
ii
DENVER, Cuîo.— -Delegates to the annual convention of*
the National Farmers Union adopted resolutions calling for a
strong farm program and heard unusually significant speeches
from world farm leaders and outstanding figures in the United
.States.
The most unique feature of the convention was an address
by F. of New#——-—----———
York City, who has not previously
ventured into the mid-West. Wagner
was invited by NFU President James
O. Patton to represent the 6,800,000
consumers in the nation's largest city
at a time when enemies of the farm
-, program are stirring up consumer
revolt.
The New York mayor, who is some
times mentioned as a Vice Presiden
tial possibility, responded with a vig
orous defense of the farmers and a
strong appeal for more utilization of
farm surpluses in the feeding of
starving people. He also asked farm
ers to understand the problems of
city people,
' This year's Farmers Union resolu
tions were not markedly different
from those of recent years. Although
proposed legislation was not men
tioned by name, the organization's
program for I960 points quite direct
ly to the support of bills by W. R.
Poage and about twenty others in the
House, as well as bills by Senators
Hubert Humphrey, Stuart Symington
and William Proxmire.
COMPREHENSIVE SYSTEM
CALLED FOR
As a means of getting "bargaining
power," the resolutions call for a
comprehensive system of commodity
programs adapted in workable ways
to the different commodities" and call
for unity among the farm organiza
tions to accomplish this goal.
"Such programs should be de
veloped for all commodities," the
delegates said, "when producers
are willing to adopt and enforce ef
, fective measures to keep market
supplies in balance with demand."
The resolutions stated that guide

Thatcher at GTA Banquet
I didn't know there were
so many ways to skin a farm
er," said Farmers Union GTA
general manager, M. W.
Thatcher, in Reviewing how he
first became interested in farm
ers and their problems, up
wards of a half century ago.
Thatcher was the principal speak
er at GTA 's 12th annual school ban
quet in Great Falls last Friday eve
ning. Upwards of 1,000 Montana
farm people heard the longtime man
ager of the regional co-op speak.
Introduced by Montana GTA man
ager Jule Waber as "the man who
has done more for the grain farmer
than any man who ever lived,
Thatcher reminisced on how he, as a
young accountant fresh out of Chi
cago, became what amounted to an
"angry young man" over the plight
of grain growers at the hands of
Twin Cities commission houses.
He recalled how, when the Non
Partisan League came into power in
North Dakota, he was able to get
laws enacted to give some protection
to that state's grain producers. How,
later on he came to Montana and
set up an accounting office in Great
Falls.
Emphasizing "you've got to have a
farm organization to build and keep
your co-operatives," the GTA head
told of his efforts to establish Mon
tana Farmers Union, and the opening
ii
%y
line for a comprehensive program
should provide for;
• Administered farm prices to be set
yearly ... by national farm income
improvement committees made up
of democratically-elected farmers;
• Establishment of national goals
sufficient for domestic and export
use requirements at prices that will
enable farmers to earn and get a
fair part of national income;
• Government^ with the advice of
farmer committees, should deter
mine the national need for farm
products, for the commercial mar
ket, domestically and for export
for dollar sales without subsidies,
plus the additional amounts that
are required by the government to
carry out domestic welfare and
foreign policies of the United
States.
Methods recommended for achiev
ing the goals include loans, farm in
come deficiency payments, marketing
agreements and orders, marketing
premium payments, stabilization op
erations, and a system of marketing
quotas.
QUOTAS AND ALLOTMENTS
Discussing the achievement of bar
gaining power by management of
abundance the resolution states;
'^Marketing supply controls should be
expressed in terms of commodity
units such as bushels and pounds or
as acreage allotments, pr both, as
required for most effective operation
of the program. Where unit market
ing quotas are used as the exclusive
supply control method, each farmer
(Continued On Page Eight)
of an MFU office in Billings in 1926.
Another big battle he recalled hav
ing am active part in was the securing
of a premium payment for farmers
on the basis of protein content of
wheat, and how success was finally
achieved in 1928. Before that time,
he said, farmers got nothing for high
er protein content, the commission
men took it all.
WITH FDR IN 1933
The next big step he said, came in
1933 on the heels of a disastrous pe
riod of years for farmers, when Pres
ident Franklin Roosevelt enlisted his
assistance and guidance in drafting
the first farm legislation of the New
Deal, and how that legislation laid
a basis for a stable agriculture which
was to endure until Ezra Taft Ben
son came into authority in the De
partment of Agriculture.
The building of co-operatives,
Thatcher said, has been a driving
force with him for more than four
decades, and he briefly recounted the
changes in co-operative management
and operatioh in the years since the
end of World War I.
"By building co-ops,'' he said, "we
build tools for farmers to use." These
tools, he continued, and the increased
income they've brought to Montana
has been good for everybody in
Montana.''

Labor To Hear,
Back Candidates
At Convention
The Montana Committee on
Political Education AFL-CIO
will hold its convention in the
Eagles Hall in Helena, April
9th, James S. Umber, president,
announced Wednesday.
The prime purpose of the
pre-primary convention will be
to screen and endorse candi
dates for state and national of
fices.
All such candidates of both
major political parties are in
vited to attend and will be giv
en an opportunity to address
the delegates.
More than 100 delegates
from all sections of the state
will be in attendance, Umber
said.
In the screening process each
candidate will be asked to state
his or her position on issues of
importance to members of or
ganized labor.
The answers to these ques
tions and any past record of a
candidate will be given careful
Consideration by the convention
before an endorsement is made,
Umber concluded.
FTC Complaints Quadruple
NEW YORK CITY—(CNS)—Fed
eral Trade Commission is getting five
times as many complaints about ad
vertising as it did before congressmen
dug into television quiz shows.
FTC's chief, Earl Kintner, said here
recently that the agency was get
ting 200 complaints a, month from
persons who cited newspaper, maga
zine, radio, and TV ads they believed
to be fraudulent. This has now risen
to 1,000.

JOSEPH C. SIBLEY,

THE TARIFF NO ISSUE.
The plea of the manufacturer for a
tariff has ceased to be a patriotic plea.
It has been claimed that the tariff
was in the interest of American labor,
but the tariff monopolists have given
the lie to that pretense. The union
between the high tariff combine of
the East and the gold combine of
New York and London has destroyed
all claim which protection had upon
the patriotism of the country. We 1
are for protection, but we desire to
protect all industries alike. East
ern manufacturers are also for pro- 1
tection, but they propose to protect a '
few monopolies and place the country 1
at large in competition with the
coolie labor of the Orient. They
also propose that the coolie shall have <
100 per cent advantage in his compe
tition with American citizens by rea
son of the difference of exchange,
which is a bounty of 100 per cent on
all the coolie exports and a tax of
100 per cent on his imports, which
compels him to manufacture for him
self and for the world at large. The
gold standard and protection is a cor
rupt combination. The gold stand
ard gives the silver standard countries
a wall of protection against the pro
ducers of America, which the favored
few who enjoy protected monoplies
insist shall continue. They want
protection for themselves to make
their products high. They want the
gold standard to make money dear.
They want the gold standard to make
all material which they manufacture
cheap, while protection makes their
commodities dear. They want the
gold standard to give them the benefit
of cheap Asiatic production of raw
material. In other words, they want
exclusive privileges in the favorite
industries they pursue, and exclu
sive privileges in the increasing pur
chasing power of the money they
loan. Since the manufacturers of the
Hast have joined Shvlock of London
to rob the people of the United States,
and made Shylock and McKinley
twin brothers in the campaign of 1890
so that no man can be a Republican
without being in favor of the miseries
of the gold standard, a vote for Roth
schild’s sham Napoleon of Ohio is a
vote for Rothschild himself, because,
without Rothschild there would be no
McKinley boom.
In 1893 when the patronage of the
government and the gold gambler’s
squeeze were forcing the repeal of the
purchasing clause of the Sherman act,
the Clevelands and the Shermans,the
Reeds and the Carlisles,the McKinleys
and the Hills,and their stock jobbing
and gold gambling associates declared
that such repeal would produce imme
diate good times and relieve all the ills
which were attiicting the country.
Did they believe what they said? If
tha answer is yes, then they were
stupidly ignorant, because they
I I
ought to have known that cutting yfT
the supply of new money which the
Sherman act furnished would make
times worse. If they did not mean
what they said'.and we do not believe
they did) what class of political huck
sters and criminals in any country
and in any age can be compared to
the infamous lies they told and the
| infamous deception they practiced
upon the American people? If it be
a fact that the gold cons pi rntors se
cured the passage of the act repealing
the purchasing clause of the Sherman
law and removing from the statute
bo»k every law which recognized
silver as money by lying to the peo
ple that such repeal would produce
good times when they knew it would
not, it would disgrace the inmales of
any penitentiary on earth to compare
them to such a gang. If they did
not know that the destruction of sil
ver would make hud tirms the impu
dence of their ignorance would put to
shame tne false prophets of nnv age,
and make their brazen assumption of
wisdom as intolerable as the crimes by
which they afflict the human race.—
Silver Knight.
The Fanners' Outlook says “'a free
silver Republican or Democrat is like
a boy with his suspenders crossed in
front—you can’t tell whether he is
going from you or coming towards
you.”

Wc have but one price. No seconds or thirds in the
canned goods we carry. Wc handle no Cheap John,auction
or old wharfage stock. Do not charge our customers in
the country 40 to 50 per cent more for goods than town
people ; thereby later on compelling them to mortgage
their places, thus depriving their dear children of the
wants and comforts of a good old home, which it took
years and years to accumulate. The house of the
“ Leaders of Enterprise stands above all this, as not one
mortgage is to their credit on the County Recorder’s
books in Humboldt County against their customers.
A suggestion.—Buy only what you need and for cash,
or as near it as possible, and from those who you know
are interested in your welfare as well as their own;
then you can rest assured you will have no bad bills to
meet, no mortgages to lose sleep over, and you can rest
contented, as an American citizen should. Our Mottj
—Live and let live, equal justice to all, then neighborly f
love will prevail in all cases.
F.C. ROBINS & GO.

Thursday, August 26, 1937.
| WHO'S NEWS I
THIS WEEK...
By Lemuel F. Parton
. XT EW YORK.—Big, bulbous Gen.
Feng Yu-Hsiang craves action.
Among all China’s rampant war
lords, the old Christian general is
. _ the least inclined
General teng to turn the other
Doesn’t Tarn cheek, and the one
Other Cheek most conspicuous
ly free from
charges of dealing under the table
with Japan. It was he, say the
news reports, who eased Chinese
troops into the Japanese Tientsin
concession, in civilian clothes, threw
Japanese strategy into confusion
and pretty nearly wrecked it. The
peasants love him. If ever a hu
man tidal wave engulfs the invad
ing Japanese, he will be riding it.
He has been fighting, off and on,
for about 40 years, sometimes as a
regular and sometimes in more or
less private wars. The politicians
dislike him and every once in a
while have him sent off to the fog
belt. The last time was in 1929,
when he stirred up a revolt in an
effort to start an “up-and-at-em”
movement against Japan. But they
always have to call him out of re
tirement, as when Chiang Kai-shek
was kidnaped last December.
Feng always has enough loyal sol
diers in reserve to count him in in
any large-scale ruction—he has
commanded as many as half a mil
lion men. When Chiang was res
cued, he made peace with Feng and
the latter assumed full responsibil
ity for the national military coun
cil, of which Chiang is chairman.
He was born of coolie parents in
Chaohsien, Anhwei. In June, 1900,
.... he was a big hulk-
Missionary in g lad> stand _
Unscrambles ing guard in a
Snake Dream Peking compound,
where some
American missionaries had been
trapped by the Boxers. He became
friends with Mary Morrill, a mis
sionary girl from Maine. One night
he had a terrible dream about a
snake under his bed. He asked Miss
Morrill to interpret it.
What Miss Morrill made of the
dream is not recorded. A day or
two later, Feng saw a woman be
headed. He was troubled, without
knowing just why, and again saw
Miss Morrill. She converted him to
Christianity. In the following years,
the fighting trade was brisk in Chi
na, and Feng began to get a repu
tation, now as a free lance and now
with the imperial armies. He
preached to his troops evdry day.
If his men didn’t want to listen,
he used to pay them.
Each soldier wore a brassard,
pinned on with a safety pin, and
written on it the Ten Command
ments. But Feng had done a re
write job on the original. His ten
commandments gave specific direc
tions for overcoming an enemy in
different situations. Every so often,
Feng would announce that, no mat
ter what the exigencies of battle
might be, it was important for a
man to discover his own soul.
On these occasions, he would go
to the country, wearing an old pad
d ded coolie coat,
stops Battle with a vacuum
to Paint or bottle full of paint
Read Poetry brushes hung from
a cord around his
waist. He would paint plum blos
soms for a while and then squat on
his heels and read Confucius or
Lao Tze. After a few months of
this he would get back to his fight
ing.
When Chiang Kai-shek counted
him out in 1929, he went to a hovel
hung high on a rocky shoulder of
T’ai Shan, China’s “sacred moun
tain,” a place set aside for citizens
who wanted to take spiritual inven
tory. Nearby was the ancient “Hall
of the Five Sages.”
Feng used the hall as a sort of
private university. He hired a fac
ulty of seven venerable teachers, he
being the only pupil. The curricu
lum listed first a course on "the
spring and autumn period of the
Chou dynasty.”
Feng is an ascetic, abstemious in
his diet, wearing cotton coolie
clothes, subjecting himself to rigid
discipline. In his retreat on the
sacred mountain, he rose every
morning at four o’clock.
His task was to draw on each of
four sheets of parchment the four
p.. _ . most beautiful
etires Early c h a r acters h e
°t Retreat on could possibly de-
Sacred Mount His furmture
consisted of a
mat, a table and two straight
backed chairs and a tiny oil lamp.
He always wetit to bed at 8:30
o’clock, to save oil, he said, and
to be rested for his early rising.
When Miss Morrill converted
him, she made him a member of the
“Way-Way-Whay,” which means
“beautiful, beautiful society.” It is,
in essence, the Chinese name for
the Methodist church, but to Feng,
the frustrated artist, it was an or
ganization for the propagation of
beauty.
So when he isn’t fighting, he ful
fills his Christian duty, as he sees
‘t- by searching out beauty. He is
* pacifist and dislikes violence in
all forms, but before he gets back
to his plum blossoms and his Lao
1 he would like to get just one
good big walloping clout at Japan.

HE story goes that many years ago a progressive merchant in
Ithaca, New York, wanting to build up his Sunday ice cream
business, supplied a “topping” on Sundays without extra charge.
This Sunday topping or “Sundae” brought him much business but also
the demand for the same thing on week days, too. And soon all his
competitors were following him—and in time all over America, soda
fountains came to feature “Sundaes.”
And now at home, the sundae is all the vogue. There are many
sundaes, but here is probably the prime favorite and made in this
quick and easy way, “there’s nothin’ it it”—
CHOCOLATE SUNDAE
2 squares of unsweetened 11-3 cups condensed milk,

Melt chocolate in top of a double boiler. Add sweetened condensed
milk and stir over boiling water for five minutes until mixture thick
ens. Add salt and then stir in hot water to the thickness desired. This
makes about 2 cups of sundae.
A “dab” of whipped cream or a sprinkling of chopped nuts are
nice additions, while pecan halves are “tops”. Home made ice cream
is delicious and economical, but the container from the store is given
a more home like effect when a home made sundae is added.

Dining Abroad
At Home
The French Peasant’s Method
Of Preparing: Sausages For
Sunday Night Suppers
The sausage used is home made
pork sausage meat in casings—
about half the size of the Ameri
can frankforter quantity, one
pound for four. Place in an iron
frying pan with a small amount
of fat, if necessary, and cook until
about half done. Then sprinkle
with a little flour. Then add half a
cup of dry white wine and two
tablespoons cream. Simmer until
sausages are done, seasoning to
taste with salt and pepper, if
needed. (A chicken liver or two, if
available, may be dropped in for
about five minutes of simmering.)
Have ready pieces of left over
stale bread which have been fried
in butter. Place slices of bread on
hot platter and place sausages on
top, dressed with the sauce. Garn
ish with parsley or water cress.
Pickled beets or spiced water
melon rind pickle are appropriate
condiments and a mild cheese of
the petit gruyere type with good,
black coffee, rounds out a neat
supper of a distinctively foreign
design.

And Jasper Women
Include Recipes
Additional foreign recipes con
tributed by home department mem
bers include the following:
Spanish Beans
1 cup of pink beans
1 large onion
1 or 2 lbs. pork (shoulder)
3 cups cooked tomatoes
1 teaspoon each of salt and sugar
Black pepper and cayenne pepper
Chile to taste.
Par boil beans. Cut pork in small
pieces and boil about one hour. Add
beans and onion, salt to taste and
cook until beans are beginning to
get soft. Add other ingredients and
cook until all is quite done. After
adding tomatoes, cook over very low
fire and do not let scorch.
This is especially good to serve
in winter with sweet potatoes and
brown bread and butter, in summer
it is good with cottage cheese.—Mrs.
S. W. Johnston, Spruce-Westmor
land.
oo
Boast Duck
(The National Bird of Bohemian)
Roast same as we do chicken. Add
a few caraway seeds. Just a pinch.
These give a delicious flavor to the
bird.—Mrs. Louis Krall, Jasper.
oo
Potato Dumplings
10 medium potatoes boiled with
skins on. Remove skins and mash
8 cups flour
1' •! tbsp. salt
Mold into dumplings.
Cook in boiling water until done,
about 15 to 20 minutes depending
upon the size. Baking power may
be used. Must be served immediate
ly while they are light and fluffy.
They become tough and soggy if
allowed to stand.
This recipe makes 36 dumplings.
—Mrs. John Fiala. Jasper.

Ginger Rogers adopts Chinese coolie hat with veil.
Wic-? Range of Hats
Decreed by Fashion
By LISBETH
Hats for the season range all the
way from the most informal berets
and visor turbans for sports, to the
largest and most picturesque of pic
ture hats trimmed with flowers or
feathers. Beach hats tend to go
comic, with high peaked crowns like
Chinese coolie hats, or with no
crowns at all. And the Chinese in
fluence also extends to “dress hats”
—decidedly so.
Peaked crowns that give height
should be worn only by women who
are young and of a certain type, or
the wearer may appear ridiculous.
Knowing what one can and cannot
wear is half the battle in choosing
a smart looking chapeau, you know,
and these "sugar loaf” hats are not
becoming to every one, I warn you.
Neither is the type that swirls
down over one side of the head in
an extreme manner; they may be
chic—are, in fact—but they are not
for every woman.
Three Chosen
I have chosen threi-models that
are generally becoming, although
you may question the enoice of the
one in the center, worn by Ginger
Rogers. About the model on the
left, modeled by Bette Davis, there
is no question. It is very smart
and wearable.
Bette’s hat is an oyster colored
felt. The brim is wide and stiff,
and is designed to keep the glaring
sun from getting into Bette’s lovely
eyes. The band is of grosgrain rib
bon, and is run under felt inser
tion. Worn at a pert angle, this
Barbara Tondro Leaves Tuesday
To Be Married in Philadelphia
Miss Barbara Tondro, daughter of
Mr. and Mrs. W. A. Tondro of El
Centro, will leave the city Tuesday
night for Philadelphia, Pennsyl
vania, where she is to be married to
John Fletcher Frankish. Miss Ton
dro will arrive Sunday in Philadel
phia and the marriage will take
place shortly afterwards.
The marriage is the culmination
of a college romance at U.S.C. where
both were students and both widely
known, being active in many cam
pus organizations.
Miss Tondro was a member of the
June graduating class, her sorority
being Zeta Tau Alpha. She is also
for speech and music. Phi Beta.
Mi . Frankish is a member of the
class of 1935 and is affiliated with
LEADING CREWS
WILL VIE AT
POUGHKEEPSIE
POUGHKEEPSIE, N. Y„ June 22.
(U.R) —The lure of modern “galley
slaves" powering their slim racing
shells down the Hudson river in one
of the most gruelling of all sporting
events drew 80.000 persons to this
sleepy valley town today.
They came to see 18 frail cedar
boats manned by 144 under-gradu
ate sweep-swingers individually
the most unknown figures in sport
—clash in three races of the 37th
inti J "ollegiate rowing association
regatta.
Crews from California, Cornell,
Washington, Syracuse, Columbia,
Pennsylvania and Navy will start
in the varsity race with the two
rugged west coast boatloads and
Cornell’s “Big Red” crew favored to
wage a savage light for the trophy.
type of hat may accompany sev
eral summer outfits, and will prove
as valuable an addition to any
wardrobe, or the bride's trousseau
as it is to Miss Davis.
Another equally wearable model
is shown right. It has a wider
brim than Bette’s, so I suppose
will keep that much more sun from
spoiling its owner’s complexion, and
it is worn here with the "pax” silk
print dress that was designed es
pecially to promote peace, the word
pax being Latin for peace. It was
conceived by Charlotte Brate, a
member of the board of directors of
the Women’s International League
for Peace and Freedom, and has
that organization’s approval. It is
a blue and white silk sheer with
PAX the symbol of the design.
The dress has a soft pleated bertha
collar edged in white and a white
flower at the neckline. An all
white hat, simple in line, is the
correct accessory.
Ccolie Enters Picture
The humble Chinese coolie comes
into the picture with Ginger Rogers’
head covering. It’s a- freak style
and I wouldn't recommend it to a
girl who hadn’t several hats to alter
nate with it, as one would be apt
to grow a bit weary of it if worn
continuously. But it is a smart little
"lid” nevertheless; for the pretty
woman who likes to be a bit .ultra
in her dress. It is made to fit se
curely on the hair behind, and the
veil gives a soft effect to the face,
tl’s becoming to Ginger, anyway.
Sigma Nu fraternity. In addition,

cieties, a few of which are the Tro

ma Delta Chi. the latter being an
honorary journalistic fraternity.
Mr. Frankish is connected with
the Philadelphia bureau of the Unit
ed Press and has done distinguished
newspaper work already, having had
a number of important assignments.
For the present the young couple
will make their home in Philadel
phia.
Miss Tondro has selected for her
wedding dress a smart sport suit in
white in keeping with the bridal
tradition.
The marriage is of valleywide in
terest due to the prominence of the
bride’s family.

El Centro C of C
Favors Sales Tax
For Post-War Fund
(Continued from Page 1)
CtreCi aiiu luxther
sewer constructs n.
That the funds must be raised
was generally conceded by direc
tors, and the merits of live possible
devices were discussed. They are:
1. City bond issue. Generally con
demned, on grounds that only 75
to 80 per cent of the tax dollar
goes to pay cff debt, the balance
being used to defray interest costs.
2. Occupational tax increase. Con
sidered insufficient to meet need.
3. Gross income taxes to be levied
on all city businesses. Agreed as
being expensive to administer.
4. Occupational tax levied on basis
of number cf employes. Fluctuating
payrolls due tc seasonal business
make this plan impractical.
5. City sales tax.
IN OTHER CITIES
Great interest centered about the
city sales tax method newly inaug
urated in San Bernardino. This
plan, it was reported, is proving
highly successful in raising funds
for elaborate postwar projects, and
is being universally accepted by the
population.
Inasmuch as it might, if adopted
only in El Centro, send some trade
to other valley communities which
otherwise would come here, it was
proposed that other cities be con
tacted with the thought of all val
ley towns considering the plan.
A meeting with the Brawley
Chamber of Commerce was sched
uled for Monday night, the meeting
having been requested by the
Brawley group, according to presi
dent Brampkamp.

The espionage occurred against

in late February. Those executed
were Heinz Fetry, 16, and Josef
Schoner, 17.

1* Transportation can create wealth just as surely as
iaiming oj manufacturing. For it increases the value of a
H»oauCi: by taking it from where it exists in abundance to
where it is scarce and needed. Therefore, the more product
you can move, the more wealth you can create-and the more
> ou can earn.
2e Both the Chinese coolie and our Union Oil truck
drivers are transportation workers. Yet the coolie makes only
5c a day and our truck drivers average $9.90. Why? Well,
the coolie, with his equipment, can only transport 10 gallons
of keros: .e about 20 miles in a day.
3e But our trucker, with the machinery at his disposal,
can transport 5,000 gallons 300 or 100 miles in a day. Conse
quently he can create many times as much wealth untn his day s
work. In other words, mechanization - not the oopular fallacy
that China is "overpopulated” - accounts or .he difference.

4e The average American can’t produce much more with
his bare hands than the average Chinese-or European. But
he has far more machinery at his disposal with which to multiply
the labor of his hands. At Union, for example, there is $43,525
in equipment-oil wells, trucks, etc.-for each employee.
5e Thl* equipment is owned by 32,227 Union Oil stockhold
ers. But who owns it isn’t so important as the fact that it is
available for use. For while the stockholders’ dividends last
year amounted to 3% on net capital invested in equipment,
the employees were able to earn far more than that from the
use of the equipment.
So the important point is not who owns the machines
of production but under what system will those machines be
best and most plentiful. Our free, competitive American system
isn’t perfect yet, but it has put more and better machines at
our disposal than any other system known to man.

minor house amendment and sent
to President Truman a bill to ex
tend the statute of limitations for
prosecutions in connection with the
attack on Pearl Harbor Dec. 1941.

Democrats of Imperial county
will meet at the Planters hotel in
Brawley at 7 p.m. Friday for their
regular monthly dinner session and
program.

businessman of Imperial county,
will speak on “Reconversion and
Post-war Planning.” Leverette
House of Brawley, program chair
man of the Democratic county cen
tral committee, is in charge of ar
rangements.

POST-PRESS CORRECTS
ERROR ON ANNULMENT
Reestablishment of his marriage
to Nettie B. Cash Pickford is being
sought by Aaron Garrett Cash in
the civil complaint filed here Fri
day, rather than annulment of his
marriage as erroneously stated in a
news story in the Sunday Post-Press.
The complaint states that Mrs.
Pickford divorced Cash in Nevada
April 30, 1945, and married John
Jackson Pickford May 1, 1945 in
Yuma. It petitions the superior court
to declare her divorce from Cash,
as well as her marriage to Pickford,
as null and void.
In the complaint. Cash sets out
that the Nevada divorce is illegal on
the grounds that his wife was not
a resident of Nevada.
The Post-Press desires to correct
the errors of the previous story.

SUFFRAGISTS JUBILANT

WASHINGTON, D. C.. Jan. 9.—
A bitter fight is expected on the
floor of tho House tomorrow at
which since the suffrage amend
ment to the Constitution comes up
far a vote.
The suffragists arr confident of
victory and ore jubilant over the
fact that they are already sure of
273 votes for the Amendment as
against 110.
As it is only necessasry that the
Amendment to have a two thirds
majority vote to pass tj become c.
part of tho Constitution its sup
porters rxe possive of its passage.
This Amendment fills a long fetl
wr.nt by a democratic people and
every fa:r minded man and woman
in the United States will be glad
(liat the law; makers of the country
have at last seen fit to grant co the
wamen at this time when they are
laboring so sincerely by nursing
ar.d assisting lr. all other ways to
make this war a success. Having
gained this right at this time the
women of the United States can
surely sty that this is a war for de
macrr.cy ard we heartily agree, ’t
should have been done long ago.
, WASHINGTON, D. C., Jan. 9.—
All the trains arriving to day were
filled with women suffragettes
from all pr.rts of the country who
in showing up in big number will
impress the Congressmen to vote
the Amendment in their favor.
The Chief of Police says no demon
strations will be allowed. But it
is expected that the women will
chow up in great numbers in tjie
front of the Houbc of Congress.

AMSTERDAM, Jan. 9.—A hea<
on collision occurrod between i
German troop train and munltoli
train w.hile speeding along at thi
rate of 46 miles an hour, near th<
town of Kaiscrlautern in Bavaria
The wreckage caught fire mak
ing it a very difficult problem fo:
the relief party to assist the injur
ed and recover the dead. Over fort;
[ Ucr.d bodies have been recovered so i
i far, and one hundred and sixty two
i seriously injured soldiers have been
i rescued from the burning wreck.
> ' The cause of-the disaster was the
, overlapping of rush orders while
• the troops were been taken from
■ Neustadt to Homberg.
Kaiserdautcrn is a City of 65,000
r population and is situated on the
banks of the Lantern river about
86 miles in a southwesterly direc
tion from tfcc great military City
of Metz. History says that in 1793
and 4 the French army suffered

the war are being scrutinized care
fully by experts in the Bureau of
Foieign and Domestic Commerce,
of the Department cf Commerce,
and the recent important report on
the subjoct of “Gorman Foreign
Trade Organization’ by Chancey D.
Snow, which touched on this point,
has been in such great demand as
to establish a new sales record for
Government trade reports.
Since the war eliminated Germany
from world trr.de, the United States
has improved lt3 position in foreign
m-rkots ir. spite of the shipping
shortage ard other abnormal re
strictive factors. Department offi
cials point out that the future pros
perity of the country will deper.r'. j
in part on Germany's loss of good
will in practically all foreign mark
ets and in part upon American abil
ity to maintain our new prestige.
American business must net make
nrsiako of seeing only one aide of
this situation and thus underesti
mating the German capacity for
foreign trade.
In order to understand the signi
canco of the information cn Ger
man trade moves that will from
limo to time be made ptiblic, Secre
'..ary Rcdfield counsels not imitation
of, but familiarity with tho Gorman
theories of foreign trade and the

IMPORTATION COOLIE LABOR
OPPOSED BY LABOR UNIONS
SAN FRANCISCO, Jan. 9.—
During a lengthy conference held
hero today by Fruit Growers and
Labor Agents, a telegram was re
ceived from Washington D. C., to
the effect that under no circum
stances whatsoever would the Fed
oral Authorities permit the importa
tion of Chinese Coolie Labcr
through any Pacific Port. The con
ference through the San Francisco
branch of the State Council of De
fence Immediately wired to Wash
ington D. C., asking the authorities
to grant permission to import Mex
ican Coolie Labor during the period
of the war.
The Manufacturers and Merchants
Association of San Francisco have
been urgently insisting upon Con
gress to pass special legislation, so
the Coollo Labor could be brought
into tlio Country.' All Labor orga
nizations on the Pacific Coast are
strenuously opposing the introduc
tion of any such measure.
•The pretext as to the scarcity of
Labor i3 merely a subtefugo to dis
rupt Labor Organizations.

MILES ARRESTED
ESPIONAGE ACT
LOS ANGELES, Jan. 9.—Marbet
Miles who was rrrcsted some time
ago by the Federal Authorities for
an alleged violation of the espion
age act, and who has
been detained by the
Military authorities at Fort Mc
Arthur was released by the U. S.
Judge of tho Federal Court of the
Southern District of Califcrnia.
The Court concurred with Mr. Miles
that the military authorities had
clearly exceeded their authority in
arresting him. Ho was re-arrested
ind charged tyith violating another
ioction of .the Espionage act. The
Federal Judge before whom Mr.
Miloo was arranged to plead empha
tically stated that he believed Miles
is the victim of a conspiracy con
cockted by army officers who seems
to havo inaugurated a system of
unlawful intimidation of law abid
ing citizens. The Judge openly an
nounced his intention of making a
thorough investigation of the mat
ter and will endeavor to succeed
in bringing about an indictment of
any Army Officer who may be found
guilty of plotting or conspiring
against citizens and will insist they
be .brought before a Military Court
martial for such an offence.
Considerable excitement has re
cently been caused by this unlaw
ful practice of some of the Military
Authorities. Miles emphaticaly de
nies any connection whatsoever
with any alleged plot to secretly
smuggle thousands of dollars worth
or arms and ammunition from the
United States into Mexico. Federal
officials claim that he is directly
connected with a gigantic conspir
acy to smuggle an abundance of
arms and ammunition across the
border and dispose of it to indi
vidual Mexicans. They say Miles
has practically admitted having in
formation that such a conspiracy
exist, although he says he has no
connection whatsoever with same.
Miles explains the re; son why ho
went to the basement of the Feder
al Building is because he was asked
by the Federal Dictrict Attorney,
Robert O’Conner and Federal
United States Marshal Thomas
Watson to accompany them to the
basement to. inspect a machine gun
which they arid was there. This
case is being watched by the peo
ple throughout the whole state.
CAMPBELL’S REASON
COAL SHORTAGE
WASHINGTON. D. C., Jan. 9.—
The sensational declaration waa
mr.de on. the floro of the House of
Congress today by Representative
Campbell of Kansas that there was
absolutely no excuse for a fuel
shortage in the Country. He said
Lhero is a plentiful supply of fuel
to be had but the present shortage
is caused by the inefficient method
of transportation due to the incom
petoncy of Fuel Administrator Gar
Beld. Tho people throughout the
Country were told last fall by Mr.
Garfield not to buy coal at that time
because the same could be bought
later on. Representative Campbell
said that many citizens were sever
ely suffering and some dying for
want of fuel to keep their bo
dies warm. The untimely and un
wise advice given by Garfield last
fall has developed into a bitter feel
ing against him, and a movement
is said now being made for his re
moval as a National Fuel Admi
nistrator. Many of the coal mines
throughout the Country are work
ing only half shifts on account of
the shortage of cars to transport
the coal from the -mines to the con
sumers.
This deplorable condition of af
fairs ccmbinod with some of the
arbitrary rulings of such a dictator
Is considered to be good and suffi
cient grounds for his removal. Gar
field has adviced the Mine Owners
to get the miners to sign contracts
co that the Government will back
the companies in the enforcement
of the men living up to such con
tracts as may be signed by them. It
was further suggested that a clause
bo Inserted to the efTect that all
men laying o5 without a good and
sufficient reason be fined the sum
of $1.00 per day during such time
ns they remain away from work.
The ccal miners ere unar.umously
opposed to the signing of any such
contracts.

ately. It is confidently expected

text:
’’Oil for the Lamps of China" ♦ ♦ By Alice Tisdale Hobart
Western Life, Love Clash With
Ancient Ways of the
Far East
(This is a serialization of the Cosmopolitan production
coming to Warner Brothers' Earle, June 14J
WHAT HAS GONE BEFORE
After three years in Manchuria as a field man for an
oil company Stephen Chase, disappointed by his Amer
ican sweetheart, Lucy, marries Hester Wentworth whose
father had died on the trip to China.
They live in a lonely company hut in Manchuria.
Stephen’s duties keep him from home much of the time.
The company adopts his plan to sell cheap lamps.
Hester loses her baby at birth. They return to the
“States” when Stephen gets his vaction. Stephen is no
tified at the home office that he is to have charge of a
division.
Ho, a venerable Chinese, becomes his adviser and aids
him in understanding the psychology and customs of his
countrymen. Hester’s offer to teach one of Ho’s family
to speak English precipitates a quarrel in Ho’s household.
Stephen and Ho on a journey to the interior find evi
dence of thefts by one of the native merchants. Members
of the man’s family promise to make restitution.
In Ho’s eyes, Stephen realized, there was an implication in
his refusal to accept the ring—a hidden insult. He had ac
cused Ho of wanting to bribe him! Squeeze—he had accused
his friend of it all unaware he had stumbled into this di
lemma, and he must go farther; the ring and the silk must
not stay in his keeping, or it would mean that he had ac
cepted them by that indirect method so beloved by the
Chinese. So he took the ring and the silk and performed
the last ruthless act, thrusting them into the hands of his
friend, in order that Ho might know beyond a doubt that he
would not accept them.
Then at the moment when he
felt that their friendship was
destroyed, away out occurred to
him. The barriers of their two
ways of thought had erected
themselves so suddenly, that he
had forgotten there was a solu
tion.
An Impasse
“Ho, my friend!” he cried,
eagerly. “I can accept cigars,
cigarettes! And I will accept,
I will accept.” But Ho said
nothing; only snorted like a
great porpoise. Then he turned
and went out so hastily that the
skirt of his long gown blew out
behind him.
Stephen was unhappy, unable
to take satisfaction even in the
jade bracelet, his Christmas gift
for Hester, which he felt, a hard
substance, in his pocket. Would
the compromise he had suggest
ed save Ho’s face sufficiently to
reestablish the relationship?
Nothing, of course, could heal
the disappointment of Ho over
the ring—or his own disappoint
ment, for that matter. He would
have liked to own the ring, to
have worn it as a token of Ho’s
friendship.
That evening, as he went
about his dressing, in prepara
tion for the Christmas party, his
spirit was heavy. He could think
only of the loss of his friend.
Not wishing to talk, he hur
riedly completed his dressing
and went out, leaving Hester.
“Damn the company’s ruling,”
he said. “Why can’t they trust
us to decide for ourselves?”
Then Kin entered, presenting
the card of Ho, and behind him,
carrying a tray loaded high
with cylindrical boxes of cigar
ettes, came the coolie. Behind
him followed the office coolie,
carrying a tray stacked with
boxes of cigars. Behind him
was the cook with another stack.
And behind him the outside
coolie, with another.
Gravely, Stephen received
these offerings, ordering one of
the tables to be cleared, so all
could be piled upon it. Then
he gave Kin his card to send to
Ho, and, hastily wrapping
money in the remains of some
red paper Hester had been
using, he told Kin to give it to
Ho’s servants who had brought
the gifts. Thus carefully did he
keep the custom.
But when Kin had gone he
burst into wild laughter, not at
all like his own laughing.
Creighton, hearing the unaccus
tomed noise from the Chase
apartment, ran up the stairs,
poking his nose in at the door.
“By golly,” he exclaimed, “are
you starting a cigar store? I’ll
lend you one of the boys for a
Red Indian to stand outside.”
“Go and see what you’ve got,”
said Stephen. “You’ll need your
own Red Indian.”
He had seen at once what Ho
was about; he had spent for
cigars and cigarettes the amount
of the two beautiful presents—
only by spending as much as
they cost could he free himself
from the implication of squeeze,
only thus could he get back his
face.
Through the winter, forced
forward by the combined efforts
■w
NOW READ ON
♦
of the staff, the sales climbed
toward the four-million mark.
The business was at its height in
these winter months, when the
gray rain brought darkness early
into the windowless huts of the
towns and cities. Then the de
sire for light grew. Men bought
lamps and oil to push back the
evil spirits lurking in the
shadows. Shops bought lamps.
The makers of firecrackers gave
a lamp to each woman and girl
working on the night shift;
when the oil was burned out,
then the stint of labor was done
and the workers might creep
home. The modem schools
bought lamps for their students
—boys and girls reading books
from the West, reading the
words of Rousseau and Marx.
Patriarchal -families bought
lamps, and by their lights girls
like Shiao Pao read with flushed
cheeks of individualism and
even of women’s rights.
Black Mirrors
It was spring, the moment
of the Green Valley’s greatest
beauty. In the black mirrors of
the flooded paddies, the young
blades stood in serried lines of
vivid green, in the morning and
evening, each blade throwing’ its
thin shadow on the mirror of
the paddy. The valley floor a
succession of such mirrors, each
framed with green dike paths.
It was after closing hours and
Stephen was alone in his office.
Stephen had just finished his
records of work, preparatory to
turning them over to Creighton,
who was to be the new manager.
He had brought the line of his
graph up to the desired total.
Only two days before, the sales
report had shown him he had
reached the four-million mark.
Memorable years, for he had
lengthened the distance be
tween himself and that haunt
ing dread of all men—failure,
that bitter, disintegrating acid
he had seen at work in one
form in his father, another in
Jenkins, and still another in
Bumham. He was waiting for
Kin to return from the street
where he had sent him to buy
one of the primitive peanut oil
lamps. He had a fancy to take
one of them back with him to
America. Show what trade had
done. Ah, there was Kin!
“Master,” said Kin, “I have
been throughout the city. It
can not be bought. It is no
longer desired by the people of
the city. But they say that
they can send to some village
and get the master what he
wishes.”
The smoky lamps no longer
even made in the city! Stephen
continued to sit tipped back
in his swivel chair, his hands
behind his head. At that
moment he looked very young
—like the boy who had first
come to China. His dreams,
so long forgotten, covered up
in the fierce drive to make
sales, all coming back. The
dream of the little lamps, that
beautiful ardor which had
come to him the night he had
tended Li Tsu’s little mistress—
dream of progress.
(To Be Continued)

text:
“Oil for the Lamps of China" ♦ + By Alice Tisdale Hohart
Western Life, Love Clash With
Ancient Ways of the
Far East
'(This is a serialization of the Cosmopolitan production
joon to be shown at Warner Brothers’ Earle Theater.)
‘ WHAT HAS GONE BEFORE
After three years in Manchuria as a field man for an
oil company, Stephen Chase planned to marry Lucy, his
American sweetheart, during his furlough.
Lucy finds Stephen a veritable stranger and refuses
to marry him. He then marries Hester Wentivorth,
whom he met in Yokohama. Her father had died on the
trip to China.
They live in a company hut in Manchuria but Stephen’s
'duties keep him from home. The company adopts, his
plan to sell cheap lamps.
Hester loses her baby at birth and returns to the
“States” ahead of Stephen. They return to Manchuria,
where Stephen has been given charge of a division.
Ho, a venerable Chinese, becomes his adviser and aids
him to understand the psychology of his countrymen.
NOW READ ON
“We want to hear all about what you’ve been doing,’’ said
Mrs. Breckenridge, the tall, thin wife of the manager of the
British Oil Company, as they entered the room. “Robin wrote
that that husband of yours doesn’t play fair, works all the
time. He daren’t take a vacation because of him.’’
, When the callers had gone she gave pleased attention to the
► house coolie pushing a cloth over the high polish of the native
varnished floor, leaving the space between Hester and him
>elf gleaming like a dark red mirror. He had restored the
gleaming perfection of the mirror when Hester came in again
from the hall with Stephen.
“What is it, Stephen?” ♦
■ When she had met him in
•the hall her mind had taken
’hold of some difficulty within
him which had not been there
'in the morning.
“Come in here,” he answered
her, passing on into the greater
privacy of their bedroom. “I
'can’t get their cooperation, not
*even Boynton’s this time. If
they don’t do it with under
standing it won’t be any good.”
“What is it you want them to
-do, Stephen?”
“I couldn’t explain it to you,
I mean, I couldn’t now. It goes
’back . .
“Perhaps if we had them to
'tiffin, you could make them un
'• derstand. I’ll sent a chit right
now.”
As they took their places at
’ the table there was a con
strained movement. Then Hes
.■iter began searching out the dis-
• eonances of the group. She drew
.forth the youthful self-assurance
of Fenton; and lest to Stephen
.he seemed insolent in his cock
sureness, she turned the theme
"away from business, to allow
him to talk of his fiance in
America.
•- Now the conversation swung
"comfortably back to their work
-and to the discussed problem.
Boynton, sitting at Hester’s right
•- hand, half turned in the chair,
forgetting her in his interest in
-what Stephen was saying.
“You see,” Stephen began,
“this plan is aimed at making
another class of Chinese want
light. Whats got to be done is
to get small tradesmen out in
the country districts thinking
about light, wanting light.
“To get the business I want
you’ve got to imagine the state
of mind of men who count
their day’s business in cash —
• cash, a two-thousandth of a
dollar.”
“Well, I guess it’s a sound
Idea,” Boynton conceded.
“We’ve been shooting too
.high.”
Long before, Hester had let
jgo their talk, her mind idling
outside the orbit of their con
-7 versation. And a new sound,
'heard for the last hour, but
hardly noticed by the weight
of its repetition at last bore
down upon her. “Hai-yo-oh, hai
i yo-oh, High-low-o, high-low-o!”
- A great weight thudding down,
..Accenting the chant made by
the jarred breath of the lab
orers.
“Ho’s begun his foreign
house,” said Stephen, breaking
into the theme of his own con-
• yersation.
Kin came and spoke softly to
'Hester, telling her a new curio
•<anan had come.
She made a little shift of her
• chair, essaying her departure.
1 Stephen looked at his watch
and rose quickly.
“I had no idea it was open
ing time.”
New Markets
For a moment they all paused
at the window, looking down
at the preparations for Ho’s
.three-story brick house. A cor
nier of the garden wall had
been tom away, a scaffolding
of bamboo poles had been
erected. Four men stood upon
\ a platform, ropes In their
hands.
2 “Hai yo-oh, hai yo-oh.” The
‘human pile-drivers hauled up
the "enormous wooden hammer,
dropped it.
“Look at them, millions just
like them in this country, lit
tle more than animals,” said
Fenton. “Do you expect us to
make them want light?”
“Yes,” said Stephen, “that’s
our market.
As the men went away to
their work, Hester knelt by the
pack of the peddler, idly
watching him take out his
wares.
“TelPhlm to take it all away.
Kin,” she commanded, pushing
from her lap a little pile of
objects she had half thought
she would buy.
< “Tai-tai,” the old man spoke
DfwHct rooo
softly, picking up a cloth-cov
ered gray box, pulling the inch
long bone spear of its fasten
ing thrust through a loop of
red braid, “look see.”
In a shaped space fitting it
accurately there lay a perfect
piece of porcelain. An artist
had made that, Hester knew.
An artist of centuries ago, for
the making of such porcelains
is a lost art. She coveted the
vase.
Quickly she turned her atten
tion to a vulgar brass image of
the Laughing Buddha, cun
ningly expressed interest in it
and none in the treasure she
coveted, thinking to lessen its
price by her indifference.
Kin, watching the old man
sitting back on his haunches
bargaining with Hester over the
Laughing Buddha, detected a
slight movement of her hands
toward the valuable porcelain.
He moved his foot a trifle, in
dicating to the peddler that it
was there the tai-tai’s interest
lay.
No, said Hester, she did not
want the image. But what of
this other thing, this bit of
porcelain? It it were not too
dear she might take it.
When the curio man stated
his price she rose and walked
away.
“You no wanchee anything?”
he called after her.
But when Hester returned,
the bowl stood upon the table
where she had planned to place
it. When she summoned Kin,
asking the reason, he said the
man had insisted on leaving it
a day for her to look at.
Suddenly the afternoon was
vivid with interest for Hester,
a new idea forming in her
mind. Everything in this room
beautiful, the other rooms, too.
Things which only the privi
leged manager’s wife could own.
She reached out covetous hands
for the vase. She must have it
at any cost. In the days that
followed, with excited energy
she offered her spirit one lovely
thing after another.
Chapter 23
Day by day Ho’s foreign
house grew taller, the scaffold
ing of bamboo poles, tied to
gether with hempen strands,
standing so high that the red
flags on their tips, placed there
to keep away the devils, floated
level with Hester’s windows.
One day after Stephen and
Ho had ended the morning’s
business, Ho spoke. “Tong ja,
there is a little matter that
pertains to my family.”
Stephen was startled into at
tention. Ho speaking of his
family to him, who had never
been beyond the garden of the
great house!
“The wife of my third
50n...”
Ho considering his daughter
in-law! There was a change
coming over him since the death
of his old mother. First the
foreign house, and now speaking
to Stephen of the women of his
household.
Occasionally Ho had spoken
of this or that son. His oldest
son, Stephen knew, was a disap
pointment; and that the third
son was his fdvorite. Once Ho
had brought this third son to
the office. He had seemed an
odd mixture of gentleman and
coolie; a thin face, aristocratic
in shape, the mouth heavy and
the nose as flat as a Negro’s, the
nose of a coolie. But the eyes
were what impressed Stephen—
honest, faithful eyes, strangely
revealing in their expression for
a Chinese. Ho’s third son was
tall, too, like his father. Stephen
wondered what the wife of this
boy of seventeen would be like.
“My son talks of the school
in the city, where girls learn.
Day and night, he does not
cease to speak of it for his wife.
It seems it is becoming the cus
tom. Perhaps good, perhaps
not good. He wishes her to learn
your speech."
.(To Be Continued),

THK HKTTEli WAV.
In the acrimony that has started
•>iu i- the war began between the
various nations, it is worth nt ting
that it is the non-combatants who
-Toit have been the bitterest m their
.iiv.re of the enemy.
.M'Giil. whose story of the Great
Bush was tlie first really great story
written about actual war by an ac
Taai fighter comments with son e
gentle sarcasm concerning the lift
eruess and the Itate which was
noticeable not in the trenches bu
in the comfortable drawing room;
of the English well-to-do, comfort
ably at home and not altogether
finding the war profitless. In and
between the trenches there was no
such sentiment and if the men had
to light, they fought as part of their
duly, fiercely and violently but with
out malice to the foe.
Very recently in England there
w.us an instance which will bear out
all this. After the German torpedo
destroyer flotilla had made an in
effective raid upon Dover and had
lost to the Dritisli torpedo destroyer
squadron, many of the German
sailors who had gone down with
their ship were washed ashore. The
bodies w. re interred with their Eng
lish comrades and enemies, each one
wrapped in tlie flag of his native
r«.-uud, and wreathes placed upon
v'jw.h from the Admiral of th Eng
firJta squadron signed “To a brave
and gallant enemy”. There was no
malice; not bitterness, but sympathy
for the men who bravely played
iinri bravely lost.
No higher tribute could be paid
to the eourage of the British seamen
fhan this tribute which they paid
with an entire absence of bitterness
to the German sailors. For it is
■only tlie cowardly and those posses
sed of abiding fear of an enemy
that can discover no good in those
who oppose them.
ft is a great pity that human be
ings would not more carefully con
sider the facts of those emotions
which they manifest, and how such
expose really what they would above
all things conceal.
It required no professional
psychologist to explain that when
a Hymn of Hate is written, favored
sind sung concerning any nation, it
betrays the fact that those who do,
acre in mortal fear of that nation.
And when hate, bitterness and accu
sations without any attempt to exte
nuate are permitted, it is a sign
that those who indulge in these
•emotional manifestations are merely
manifesting the fact that the enemy
that they attack by word and by
writings is one who has occasioned
not a little dread.
U is because the men fighting in
ribe trenches are practically devoid
*.1 fear of the enemy they will be
without bitterness and without hate
of the enemy. It is because the
British admiral and his sailors do
not fear the German navy and its
sailors that they are ready to pay
to the bravery of the sailors a last
tribute always due to brave and gal
lant enemies.
When papers in the United StatPs
devoted themselves wholly and en
tirely to cultivating hate of G( r
many, it was because they had a
•wholesome fear of German prow
ess, and when pro-German literature
circulated in the United States ex
clusively devoted its pages to bitter
nrul vindictive attacks against
•VJroat Britain as the country whence
«onid come nothing of good, it mere
ly revealed the fact that as Ger
mans or Germanists they feared
and dreaded Great Britain above all
«Vher foes. They unconsciously paid
a tribute to the might, the daring
sajul the resourcefulness of that
-enemy who had to lie thus attacked
in order that they might keep up
their own courage.
Similarly when the British com
manders in Dublin devoted them
ticJves to the suppression of the Sein
Finn uprising in Dublin with undue
ferocity instead of a prudent clem
ency, i( was simply because th->'
were filh-d with dread of the Iri.th
rebels and desperation made tlu-m
resort to desperate remedies.
Bernard Shaw has exposed quite
fully the indecency of the North
cliffe papers in their attacks upon
the enemy, regardless of the deeds
and accomplishments which that
enemy had achieved. In point oi
fact jingoism is simply another form
of national cowardice, .endeavoring
to screw its courage to the sticking
place and needing something to st1
mulate itself.
In Africa when the De Wet rebell
ion had been put down, the rule's
in Africa not disturbed by any
alarms prepared to let bygones be
bygones, hut it was because of the
fact that they had no longer any
reason to fear the. sympathizers ot
the Free State commander.
This matter as exposed in the war
is referred to as having wide appli
cation and it is one that carries with
it considerable of a lesson for all
of us.
There is nothing so vain as vin
dictiveness in any line, and when
given way to, it is because of the
fact that the man who is made the
butt of such bitterness and such
hate is well feared also. The man
who is invariably ready to denounce
or attack or assail verbally the re
putation, the nature or the disposi
tion of any person, so that such
assaults become finally an obsession,
is merely exposing the fact that lit
is obsessed with fear of the enemy.
Generally candor will admit that
this is so, and the harder is the ef
fort to conceal it. the greater will
he the certainty that this view is
correct.
Ileal moral courage it may ho »d
milted is nothing more or less than
sincerity. It is the candor that ad
mits that it doesn't like to die. that
it does not like to be hurt, that
it does not like to fight, ‘hat it does
these things only because it has to,
and because the resulting conse
j ((lienees would be far more serious
I than if these things or any of them
j were not done. To be sore there
are the rare ones who have no such
j sensation of fear of consequences,
I whose souls under all circumstances
| are unafraid. But that is because
! of the danger being not understood,
and the mental impression of such
dangers is not strong enough to ex
cite the common emotion called fear
"He jests at scars who never felt
teh wound”, merely describes the
I ordinary attribute common to most
'■ men of physical bravery. But the
moral courage that knows the dang
| ers, dislikes them, even fears them,
but rises above the emotion knaw
1 ledge has given, refuses to be the
victim of his emotional environment,
the really brave man In fact, is that
which elicits admiration from
friend and foe, which appreciates
I foes as well as friends, and which
j places upon the grave of “brave at d
j gallant enemies” the last tribute ol
| respect aroused from an experience
| that they themselves had dared
I and were still fearlessly prepared
| despite that knowledge, to dare
again.
CHINA.
The short lived existence of the
Chinese Empire, come to earth
again, may or may not be one of
the signs or the times. The Chinese
struggle for liberty has been one of
the bloodiest in history and when the
rebels won they got merely the
husk and not the kernel. Yuan Shi
Kai, with the dexterity of few mod
ern statesmen, turned the defeat of
his Manchu masters to a victory for
himself, and although some mystery
attends his death, it is likely that it
merely ended a career which would
in any event have been the dictator
of modern China.
The poor boy emperor apparently
found his seat as uncertain as ot
late has the former Tzar and while
his fate may be shorter and sharper,
their lives have at any rate the same
ill-omened destiny.
That China as a republic is a free
country is of course only a myth.
The real rulers of China will con
tinue to be the priests and the
mandarins. And that it is a great
advantage to the masses to exchange
one steady boss for a hundred
minor bosses, is a fallacy that be
fore long they will realize is a fall
acy pure and simple.
However the republicanization ot
China once more enables it to don
the toga of immaculate demo
cracy which the imperial purple
would have given the lie to. And
for that reason it is especially fortu
nate. It may no doubt sweeten the
black fate of the coolie that he
should be a citizen instead of a
subject, but it is not likely his soul
will ever rise above his sordid sur
roundings to any mental intoxica
tion on that account. He will never
achieve that which other toilers
have achieved with so little trouble,
that particularly Christian science
view concerning liberty, persuading
themselves of being freemen when
industrially enslaved. They nut;
sing their songs at their hard labor,
certain sign of the coolie mind, but
they will not find that under man
darin any more than under Manchu
their lot is lightened one iota. That
is not just yet. But this patient, long
suffering, docile and industrious peo
ple will yet pass the same way as
the rest of the world, now progress
ing, now b9ing retarded In the gen
eral movement. That the day will
come when Mandarin will fare the
way of Manchu is clear enough. The
deletion of feudal lord, monarch
and despot is now written in the Ills
tory of many lands. Each in their
time thought that the system which
endured them and for which they
endured, was eternal. Today man
darin and monied autocrat, “calling
it freedom when themselves are
free”, believe too that their class
is for eternity and that their su
premacy will last for all time. They
are undismayed by the Sun Yat
Sens that here or there bring the
light of real freedom to the strugg
ling coolie souls, because the
power which is theirs may here and
there effect its quenching. But fin
ally there comes a light, fire that is
not to be quenched, that blazes in
hill and valley, assuring the masses
and the coolies that real freedom
has dawned upon the earth, tnat
they have reached out of the dark
ness of bondage and that the clank
of chains will no longer fret the
wearied souls of men.
Manchu and mandarin are merely
footprints in the solids of time, to
be memories of another day and to
be earnest as we!! of a better era.
For even in China any more than
anywhere else, there is no surety
that one class may govern and en
slave another without that other's
consent.
in the meantime the world pities
the boy emperor. Who will pity the
submerged masses that are still held
NO TRACE OF SLOGGER
RFPORT SEATTLE POLICE!’
SEATTLK, July 12.—Police are
unably to allay the uneasiness of wo
men over the fact that a dangerous
woman slugger. likely a serious
maniac, is at large in the city. Al
though it is certain that the police
i arc making diligent efforts to dis
I cover some clue as to the identity
of the man so far they have failed.
It is now suggested that some of them
don female garments at night and
frequent such a place as the man
has been known to infest. In this
way annexing the lunatic as such
he is believed to be.
DRIVE MAY RESULT
IN CAPTURE OF DUNKIRK
_.
LONDON, July 12.—The deter
mined effort which the Germans are
making in Flanders is the final et
fort to reach Dunkirk and to ob
tain which they have fought for
many times. Dunkirk is an import
ant base for the British and even an
approach within distance or the city
so that it could be subjected to an
intensive bombardment would re
sult in enormous munition and sup
ply losses to the British forces.

Scientific
WU AIM to qonuuci our nusmcss on cor
rect scientific principles
Wo run our automobiles scientifically.
Wo buy the best Rnnollne; wo keep our
machines properly lolled nml a Rood oup
nlv of water In the rndlntor. Wo don't
try to mako an automobile do the work of
a coal cart or n threshing machine. A
man who owns a fine car takes good care
of It, htre.) an expert chauffeur to run It,
and an expert machinist to look It over
now and then to mako suro that It Isn't
Rotting looso In the joints or losing lis
efficiency.
Wo feed our horses, cows, pigs, chlckcni
and poodlo dogs pclentlficnlly. Why not
treat ourselves as well? Hccauso wo don't
know how. Wp hao found out by study,
observation and laboratory resenrch what
Is good for domestic animals and automo-
biles: but ourselves wo hnve neglected to
4iUny.
The president of a State dairymen's as
sociation said to the writer the other clay,
"I know all about how to feed cows so as
to mako them glvo the most milk and
how to feed horses so as to get tho most
work out of them, but I confess I don't
know a thing about how to feed myself
or my children,"
This man, a scientific dairyman, owns one
of the finest herds in his State and can
speak with Intelligence and authority on
ny question pertaining to tho dairy busi
ness ; but, concerning the "science of hu
man life, he confesses lamentable Igno
rance And this Is true of the aerago man.
We have been so busy cultivating thrift
and efficiency In business and In trying to
find out what Is inside the earth and why
the people of Mars are digging canals, and
how fast .the stars aro traveling and how to
get on In tho world nnd how Ho climb a
notch higher In civilization and culture
that we have forgotten to look after our
most Important personal Interests.
Living '
Klrst of all, before, wc can achieve any
thing, wo must bo good animals. To be a
good human animal does not mem to bo a
moral brute, but to havo a good stoma,ch to
support your brain and muscles Just as does
a horse, nnd Just as a horseless velilclo has a
good carbureter.
A horse knows what to eat. Turn him
looso and he will select the kinds of grasses
and twigs that are good for him. Ho has an
Instinct a "horsn sense" that serves him
in unerring guide in tho selection of his
food.
Man has lo.t his "horse sense." He has so
long abused his sense of taste that It Is no
longer a safo guide, and having no Instinct
to guide him' man undertakes to cat every
thing that any nnlmal cats. In consequence,
dyspepsia Is universal, A man who cats a
blizzard's dinner gets Into the same sort of
troublo as a man who feeds tar. Instead of
gasoline, to his nutomobllc. Ills engine
"balks," "mlses fire," 'goes dead."
Repairs are not possible so long as the
cause remains. Vacations, "tonics" and
surgery may afford temporary relief, but
the root of the mischief remains and leads
sUadlly on to the final collapse. Yet most
chronic ailments disappear very soon when
the faults which lie back of them aro cor
rected. Health, strength, efficiency, endurance,
comfort, the capacity for enjoying life
wo may purchaso If we will. The price Is
"right living."
There Is today for the first time In the
history of the world a "science," as well
as an "art." of living. Dally this de
partment will present In plain language
tho up-to-date, scientific facts relntlng to
this sclenoe. All interested readers are
asked to send In their health questions to
"The Oood Health Question Box, caro of
tho ." Thev will be answered cither
In this column or by personal letter.

THE EAST INDIAN POPULATION
OF JAMAICA. |
The repoit of the Immmgration Depart
ment to the end of 1897 was published yes
terday. The ProrrcToß has not much to say,
siiply because there is now little on which
to comment with legard to the coolie popu- |
'aticn, There were no immigrants intro- ;
duced and none were repatriated. Those nn
der inderture are decrensing. On the Ist, |
Junuary of last year there were 1,665 on the !
looks; on the 21st. December there were }
1,528, allowing for the changes due to births |
and deaths. 'T'he latter umbered 80 while |
there were only 7 birthe. The mertality |
nevertheless wis less than in the previous |
year. There were 10 xeleased from inden- |
ture. ‘The amount of money iemitted |
to India was £173 as eompared with £246 ]
in 1896, but the number of depositcrs in
the local Savings Bank increased by |
69 with an increase also in the amount of |
deposits. ‘The year was a lemarkably quiet |
one among the coolick— 10 muwiders or rui- ’
cides, ov revicug crime of any kind oceuried, ,
while their condition continued prosperous |
and Ile zelations between them and their |
ewployers were most satisfactory. Mr. Pieou |
s thus able to write:—¢The year under ve- s
view has been one of serious depression, |
causing many sugar estntes to go out of cul- |
tivation owing to the low price of sugar and
to the diowght; but lam glad to say that the
coolies have obtained employment without |
any difficulty on the several banana pilanta- |
tions, and no cases of distress have eome un
der the cognizance of this department. It in ;
o matter of congratulation that wnder the de- |
pression which thesugar industry has passed |
during the year the coolies in Westmoreland !
have been able to labour for themselves and l
earn a good living by growing rice.” The |
total number ¢f East Indinn coolies now in |
the Island is 14, 079.—Gleaner. |
